name: Seeded Generation
description: A deterministic generation approach where a seed and content version reproduce the same generated result under the same rules.
design_notes: Seeded generation is a reproducibility contract. It needs generation_seed, content_version, rng_stream_id separation, deterministic ordering, save/load compatibility, and debug metadata so players can share worlds and developers can reproduce rare bugs.
design_purpose: Allow players, QA, and designers to reproduce generated worlds, runs, encounters, or loot outcomes.
Support shareable seeds and deterministic challenge modes.
Make procedural bugs debuggable by recording seed, generator version, and validation path.
player_fantasy: The player feels they can discover, share, and revisit a specific generated world or run.

parameters.name: generation_seed
parameters.description: Primary seed used to initialize deterministic generation.

parameters.name: rng_stream_id
parameters.description: Named substream that prevents one generator from changing another generator's output.

parameters.name: content_version
parameters.description: Version of content tables and generator rules used to interpret the seed.

parameters.name: deterministic_order_key
parameters.description: Stable sort key used before consuming random numbers from a stream.

parameters.name: validation_retries
parameters.description: Maximum repair or regeneration attempts before a fallback template is used.

edge_cases: Changing content_version can make old seeds produce different maps unless old rules are retained or migrated.
Parallel generation consumes RNG streams in nondeterministic order unless each chunk or subsystem has its own rng_stream_id.
A validation retry can accidentally consume extra random values and desync later generation.
Floating point noise or unordered map iteration can produce different output across platforms.
common_bugs: Loot generation consumes from the terrain RNG stream and changes the world when an item table is edited.
Seeded bug reports omit content_version, so developers cannot reproduce the player's map.
Save files store only generated output and lose the generation_seed needed to rebuild missing chunks.
Validation fallback uses non-seeded randomness and breaks deterministic replay.
balancing_notes: Separate streams let designers tune loot, terrain, and encounters independently without invalidating every seed.
Daily or challenge seeds should lock content_version so leaderboards are not affected by mid-event patches.
If a seed can generate a bad start, include validation and fallback rules rather than relying on players to reroll.
accessibility_notes: Expose seed text in a copyable field so players can share and report worlds accurately.
For challenge seeds, disclose major rule modifiers so players are not surprised by hidden generation constraints.

implementation_notes.unity: Use a deterministic RNG wrapper with named rng_stream_id values; store generation_seed, content_version, and chunk_coordinate in saves and editor debug overlays.
implementation_notes.godot: Keep generator functions pure where possible, passing seed and stream IDs explicitly; avoid relying on global RandomNumberGenerator state across unrelated systems.
implementation_notes.unreal: Store seed and content version in SaveGame or backend run records; use deterministic streams per subsystem and log generation steps with graph_node_id or chunk coordinates.
implementation_notes.web: Use a stable seeded RNG library or integer PRNG, sort generation inputs before rolling, and include seed/version in share URLs and bug-report exports.

example_games.title: Minecraft
example_games.note: Uses world seeds to reproduce generated worlds.

example_games.title: Spelunky
example_games.note: Uses seeded daily-style runs and deterministic level generation concepts.
